caption.text:
They called it the Paris of the East. It became a graveyard.

Ross Island, less than one-third of a square mile in the Andaman archipelago, was established as a penal colony in 1858 after the Indian Mutiny. Dr. James Pattison Walker arrived with 200 convicts and rebels. Over decades, prisoners built mansions, a Presbyterian church with Italian stained glass, tennis courts, and a ballroom for British officers. The convicts slept in leaking barracks, died of malaria and cholera, and were force-fed experimental quinine that made them injure each other.

The documented fact: of 8,000 prisoners transported to the islands, 3,500 died. A magnitude 8.1 earthquake in 1941 killed over 3,000 more and damaged the buildings. The British withdrew. The Japanese occupied and pillaged the island. In 1945, the penal colony was disbanded permanently.

Here is the gap. Every other island in the Andamans was eventually repopulated. Ross Island was not. The Indian Navy took over in 1979, but no one lives there. Massive fig trees have swallowed the bungalows whole. A ballroom where people once waltzed is now a skeleton of roots and stone.

Why does one island stay empty when everything around it recovers?

text.line_2: A ballroom. Tennis courts. All abandoned.
Every other island recovered. This one didn't.
